2WJ7-DBZ: Born in the village of Swanbourne, Buckinghamshire, John Spier carried the title of doctor and crossed the Atlantic to the British colonies. He made his life in Nansemond, Virginia, where he died in 1684 at the age of about thirty-six. Little else of his story survives, but he stands among the family's earliest colonial forebears.
9JFL-24R: A New Jersey native born in Salem, John Vance settled in Frederick County, Virginia, where he worked as a cooper, crafting the barrels and casks essential to colonial life. He earned the rank of captain and lived to about sixty-nine. His 1760 will remembered his wife Elizabeth, sons John and Alexander, and daughters Hannah Crawford and Elizabeth Matthews.
9XHM-V2M: One of colonial Maryland's most powerful men, Thomas Brooke inherited the 2,530-acre estate Brookefield as a teenager and steadily amassed over 7,000 acres. Commissioned a militia colonel, he served decades on the Governor's Council, as a provincial judge, Commissary General, and Deputy Secretary, even acting as Governor of Maryland in 1720. His Catholic and later Anglican ties saw him dismissed and reinstated as colonial politics shifted around him.
GFTD-V51: Thomas Henry Sutton Jr. was born in Talbot County on Maryland's Eastern Shore and bore the title of captain. He died in Baltimore in 1729, not yet forty-five years old. The documentary record of his life is sparse, but he remains a remembered link in the family's colonial Maryland roots.
K8HQ-6RR: Born in Virginia and named in her father Cornelius Vaughan's 1735 will as one of his youngest children, Margaret Vaughan married into the Jeter family and moved to South Carolina. Family lore recalls that during the Revolution she and her husband prospered in Edgefield District, where British soldiers seeking their buried gold captured and tortured her husband over a fire. Seizing a reap hook, Margaret is said to have charged into the soldiers to save his life.
KN3V-B53: Christopher Webb II left Crediton in Devon, England, and crossed to the Massachusetts Bay Colony, settling in Braintree. A millwright by trade, he built and maintained the mills that ground a young community's grain, and he served his colony as a military ensign. He died in 1694, the founder of a long-documented New England family line.
KZC8-88Q: William Randolph Singletary came of age in Bladen County, North Carolina, just as the Revolution erupted. He entered military service in both 1776 and 1777, rising to lieutenant in the Patriot cause. After the war he remained in Bladen County, recorded there in the 1790 and 1800 censuses, and died at Elizabethtown in 1802.
L2CC-H4R: Anna Weissin was baptized in 1695 at Sissach in the Swiss canton of Basel, where she spent her early years. In 1736 she joined the wave of Swiss and German emigrants bound for the New World, arriving in South Carolina. She ultimately made her home on the Carolina frontier, dying in Rowan County, North Carolina, in 1769.
L45Q-WQ2: Samuel Prioleau was born in Charleston, South Carolina, into a family of French Huguenot heritage, and spent his life in that port city. He held the rank of colonel and was recorded among Charleston's households in the 1790 census. He died in his native city in 1792 at about seventy-five years of age.
L62H-H2S: Remembered as the patriarch of the Gaudet family in North America, Jean Gaudet first scouted Acadia with his brother around 1610 before returning years later with his family to settle for good near 1636. A farmer and laborer, he helped found Godet Village along the river near present-day Bridgetown. He lived an extraordinarily long life, recorded at ninety-six in the 1671 census and dying in Acadia around 1678.
L7GV-X32: Abraham Whinery was born in Orange County, North Carolina, and married there in 1786, raising his family in and around Hillsborough through the turn of the century. Later in life he joined the migration westward, settling in Bedford County, Tennessee, by 1820. He bore the rank of private and died that same decade.
LBHD-CS1: Alexander Strange Sr. was born in the seafaring town of Bideford, Devon, England, and carried the honorific of knight. He crossed the Atlantic and settled in Virginia, dying at Putneys Mill in New Kent County in 1725. Few details of his life survive, but he stands as an early Virginia ancestor of the family.
LC2N-HKY: Lancaster Lovett Sr. came from Astwell in Northamptonshire, England, and built his life in colonial Virginia along the shores of Lynnhaven Parish. By 1650 he was serving his parish as a church warden, a role of trust and standing in the community. He died in Princess Anne County in 1673, leaving a will to provide for his family.
LCXD-7T9: The only son of Richard Blunt, Thomas Blunt was born in Surry County, Virginia, and came into his inheritance there as a young man in 1677. He became a substantial landholder, patenting hundreds of acres along the Nottoway River and Whitewood Swamp in territory bordering the Nottoway Indians. He died in 1709, leaving his Surry County estate to his wife Priscilla and son Thomas Jr.
LH6F-HHH: A soldier of remarkable courage, Karl Friederich D'Arensbourg served on the staff of Sweden's King Charles XII and, at the Battle of Pultava, is credited with saving the wounded king's life against the Russians. After Sweden's losses he emigrated in 1721 and by 1724 had taken up land along the Mississippi on Louisiana's German Coast. Honored as a chevalier, he lived to 1777, dying in St. Charles Parish.
LHPP-DLF: Benedict Hammack was born in Richmond County, Virginia, and lived through both the founding of the nation and its second war for independence, serving in the War of 1812 in his seventies. His ties stretched to Wilkes County, Georgia, where his will was recorded. He died in Richmond, Virginia, in 1815.
LK2S-TTV: Born in Essex County, Virginia, William Sorrell Ballard Sr. led a life that bridged colonies, with records placing him in James City and King and Queen counties as well as Andover, Massachusetts. A man of standing who held the rank of captain, he was named among officials in Virginia's public records as early as 1702. He died in King and Queen County in 1754.
LR1W-8TL: Eleanor Elizabeth Parke was born in County Carlow, Ireland, and immigrated to Chester County, Pennsylvania, in 1713. A devout member of the Quaker community, she appears throughout the New Garden Monthly Meeting records, raising children with first husband James Lindley and later Henry Jones. Remarkably, she gave birth to her last child at the age of fifty-one before the family moved on to Orange County, North Carolina.
LT3J-BXN: John Heard sailed from Chichester, Sussex, around 1634 to the Piscataqua plantations, settling at Dover, New Hampshire, and holding land at Kittery on Sturgeon Creek. A sea captain, he plied the Atlantic between England and the colonies for much of his life. His family is remembered too for his wife Elizabeth, who hid a young Indian during the 1676 troubles at Dover, an act of kindness that later spared her life during the 1689 raid.
LV7S-QCT: Alexander Hamilton Shelton was born in 1895 in Marietta, Oklahoma, and grew up in Texas, where his family was recorded in Montague County. He registered for the draft in both World Wars and spent most of his adult life in Lubbock County, with a stint at Slaton. He died in Slaton in 1963.
LWQN-HG2: Martin Dawson Sr. spent his early career in Albemarle County, Virginia, where from 1747 to 1760 he served as overseer at Shadwell plantation for Peter Jefferson, father of Thomas Jefferson. He lived an exceptionally long life, dying at nearly a century old in Amherst County. His detailed 1808 will, written when he was 'greatly stricken in years,' provided for the many children of two marriages.
LY5X-PKC: Genevieve S. L. Thomas was born in New Orleans in 1874 and remained a lifelong resident of that city. The census tracked her there across four decades, and she registered to vote repeatedly through the 1930s and into 1941. She died in New Orleans in 1964 at the age of ninety.
LZ4V-L38: Nathaniel Haven was born in 1664 in Lynn, Massachusetts Bay Colony, and lived out his days in Framingham, dying in 1746. Family records curiously list him as serving in the Revolutionary War, though that conflict began decades after his death, a reminder of how genealogical records can blur across generations.
LZFT-1R3: John J. Lovett Jr. left Aylesbury, Buckinghamshire, England, as a small child, emigrating in 1637 aboard the ship True Love. He made his life in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, serving as constable of Marblehead and settling at Beverly in Essex County. He lived to about ninety, leaving a will in 1723 that fondly remembered his grandchildren by name.
LZPJ-VF4: Elizabeth Hinds came from Bury in Lancashire, England, and was among the courageous early women who settled the Connecticut River towns. Her residences trace the colony's growth, from Wethersfield and Hartford in the 1640s to Middletown by the 1650s. She is honored by name on Middletown's Founders Rock monument, dedicated to the town's first settlers, and died there in 1697.
MCJ1-TKM: John Godfrey III was born in Newport, Rhode Island, in 1703 and carried the rank of captain. He died in Providence in 1756, a little past his fiftieth year. Though the record of his life is brief, he remains a remembered son of colonial Rhode Island.
MQ8F-XLR: Robert Harris Jr. was born in New Kent County, Virginia, in 1664, with his earliest years recorded in Yorkhampton Parish. He carried the rank of major and lived his life within colonial Virginia. By the time of his death in 1739, he had settled in St. Martin's Parish in Hanover County.
